From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British monthly music magazine

*Mojo*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| February 2008 issue of *Mojo* | |
| Editor | John Mulvey (Nov 2017–present) |
| Categories | [Music magazine](/wiki/Music_magazine "Music magazine") |
| Frequency | Monthly |
| Total circulation | 62,733 (Dec 2022) |
| First issue | November 1993 |
| Company | [Bauer Media Group](/wiki/Bauer_Media_Group "Bauer Media Group") |
| Country | England |
| Based in | [London](/wiki/London "London") |
| Language | English |
| Website | [mojo4music.com](http://mojo4music.com) |
| [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") | [1351-0193](https://www.worldcat.org/search?fq=x0:jrnl&q=n2:1351-0193) |

***Mojo*** is a [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") [magazine](/wiki/Music_magazine "Music magazine") published monthly in the United Kingdom, initially by [Emap](/wiki/Ascential "Ascential"), and since January 2008 by [Bauer](/wiki/Bauer_Verlagsgruppe "Bauer Verlagsgruppe").

Following the success of the magazine *[Q](/wiki/Q_(magazine) "Q (magazine)")*, publishers Emap were looking for a title that would cater for the burgeoning interest in [classic rock](/wiki/Classic_rock "Classic rock") music. The magazine was designed to appeal to the 30 to 45-plus age group, or the [baby boomer](/wiki/Baby_boomer "Baby boomer") generation.[[1]](#cite_note-Music_Week-1) *Mojo* was first published on 15 October 1993.[[1]](#cite_note-Music_Week-1) In keeping with its classic rock aesthetic, the first issue had [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") and [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon") as its cover stars. Noted for its in-depth coverage of both popular and cult acts, it acted as the inspiration for *[Blender](/wiki/Blender_(magazine) "Blender (magazine)")* and *[Uncut](/wiki/Uncut_(magazine) "Uncut (magazine)")*. Many noted music critics have written for the magazine including [Charles Shaar Murray](/wiki/Charles_Shaar_Murray "Charles Shaar Murray"), [Greil Marcus](/wiki/Greil_Marcus "Greil Marcus"), [Nick Kent](/wiki/Nick_Kent "Nick Kent"), [David Fricke](/wiki/David_Fricke "David Fricke"), [Jon Savage](/wiki/Jon_Savage "Jon Savage"), and [Mick Wall](/wiki/Mick_Wall "Mick Wall"). The launch editor of *Mojo* was [Paul Du Noyer](/wiki/Paul_Du_Noyer "Paul Du Noyer")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and his successors have included [Mat Snow](/wiki/Mat_Snow "Mat Snow"), [Paul Trynka](/wiki/Paul_Trynka "Paul Trynka"), Pat Gilbert, and Phil Alexander. The current editor is John Mulvey.

While *Mojo* frequently covers classic rock acts such as [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones"), [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who"), and [Paul Weller](/wiki/Paul_Weller "Paul Weller"), it has also featured many newer and "left-field" acts. It was the first mainstream magazine in the UK to focus on [the White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes"), and it continues to cover emerging acts. Modern cover stars of recent years have included [Lana Del Rey](/wiki/Lana_Del_Rey "Lana Del Rey") and [Arctic Monkeys](/wiki/Arctic_Monkeys "Arctic Monkeys"). It regularly includes a [covermount](/wiki/Covermount "Covermount") CD that ties in with a current magazine article or theme. It introduced the [*Mojo* Honours List](/wiki/Mojo_Honours_List "Mojo Honours List"), an awards ceremony that is a mixture of readers' and critics' awards, in 2004.

In early 2010, *Mojo* was involved in a controversial move by its new parent company, Bauer, to unilaterally impose a new contract on all photographers and writers, taking away their copyright, and offloading liability for [libel](/wiki/Libel "Libel") or [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") from the publisher onto the contributor. Two hundred photographers and writers from *Mojo* and Bauer's other music magazines, *[Kerrang!](/wiki/Kerrang! "Kerrang!")* and *Q*, were reported as refusing to work under the new terms.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Lists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mojo_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Lists")]

More recently, the magazine has taken to publishing many "Top 100" lists, including the subjects of drug songs (*Mojo* #109), rock epics (*Mojo* #125), protest songs (*Mojo* #126) and even the most miserable songs of all time (*Mojo* #127). To celebrate 150 issues, the magazine published a "Top 100 Albums of Mojo's Lifetime" list (essentially 1993 to 2006). The top five for this list were:

1. *[Grace](/wiki/Grace_(Jeff_Buckley_album) "Grace (Jeff Buckley album)")* – [Jeff Buckley](/wiki/Jeff_Buckley "Jeff Buckley") (1994)
2. *[American Recordings](/wiki/American_Recordings_(album) "American Recordings (album)")* – [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") (1994)
3. *[OK Computer](/wiki/OK_Computer "OK Computer")* – [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead") (1997)
4. *[Time Out of Mind](/wiki/Time_Out_of_Mind_(Bob_Dylan_album) "Time Out of Mind (Bob Dylan album)")* – [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") (1997)
5. *[Definitely Maybe](/wiki/Definitely_Maybe "Definitely Maybe")* – [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") (1994)

In 2007, the magazine set out to determine "The Top 100 Records That Changed the World". The list was compiled and voted on by an eclectic panel of superstars, including [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk"), [Tori Amos](/wiki/Tori_Amos "Tori Amos"), [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits"), [Brian Wilson](/wiki/Brian_Wilson "Brian Wilson"), [Pete Wentz](/wiki/Pete_Wentz "Pete Wentz"), and [Steve Earle](/wiki/Steve_Earle "Steve Earle"). [Little Richard](/wiki/Little_Richard "Little Richard")'s 1955 hit "[Tutti Frutti](/wiki/Tutti_Frutti_(song) "Tutti Frutti (song)")"[[5]](#cite_note-5) took the number-one spot. Richard's record beat [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' "[I Want to Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Want_to_Hold_Your_Hand "I Want to Hold Your Hand")" (second) and [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley")'s "[Heartbreak Hotel](/wiki/Heartbreak_Hotel "Heartbreak Hotel")" (third). The magazine's editors wrote that "the 100 albums, singles and [78s](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record") that made up the list make up the most influential and inspirational recordings ever made". Hailing "Tutti Frutti" as the sound of the birth of [rock 'n' roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), the editors went on to state that "one can only imagine how it must have sounded when the song exploded across the airwaves!"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The top ten on *Mojo'*s "100 Records That Changed the World" list are:

1. "[Tutti Frutti](/wiki/Tutti_Frutti_(song) "Tutti Frutti (song)")" by Little Richard
2. "[I Want to Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Want_to_Hold_Your_Hand "I Want to Hold Your Hand")" by the Beatles
3. "[Heartbreak Hotel](/wiki/Heartbreak_Hotel "Heartbreak Hotel")" by Elvis Presley
4. *[The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan](/wiki/The_Freewheelin%27_Bob_Dylan "The Freewheelin' Bob Dylan")* by Bob Dylan
5. *[Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn_(album) "Autobahn (album)")* by [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk")
6. *[King of the Delta Blues Singers](/wiki/King_of_the_Delta_Blues_Singers "King of the Delta Blues Singers")* by [Robert Johnson](/wiki/Robert_Johnson "Robert Johnson")
7. *[The Velvet Underground & Nico](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground_%26_Nico "The Velvet Underground & Nico")* by [the Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground") and [Nico](/wiki/Nico_(German_singer) "Nico (German singer)")
8. *[Anthology of American Folk Music](/wiki/Anthology_of_American_Folk_Music "Anthology of American Folk Music")* (various artists)
9. "[What'd I Say](/wiki/What%27d_I_Say "What'd I Say")" by [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles")
10. "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen_(Sex_Pistols_song) "God Save the Queen (Sex Pistols song)")" by the [Sex Pistols](/wiki/Sex_Pistols "Sex Pistols")

Other lists include a Top 50 of songs by a particular artist from time to time, usually compiled by a panel of music journalists and musicians. Featured artists have included [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd"), [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"), [the Who](/wiki/The_Who "The Who"), [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), and [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Special editions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mojo_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Special editions")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Mojo_(magazine) "Special:EditPage/Mojo (magazine)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(March 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/24/Mojo_magazine_300th_issue.png/250px-Mojo_magazine_300th_issue.png)](/wiki/File:Mojo_magazine_300th_issue.png)

Cover of issue #300

After the success of an all-Beatles issue published to mark the release of *[The Beatles Anthology](/wiki/The_Beatles_Anthology "The Beatles Anthology")* in 1995, many stand-alone, special editions of *Mojo* have been produced, devoting an entire magazine to one artist or genre. Three of the most successful were the series (produced by then special editions editor [Chris Hunt](/wiki/Chris_Hunt "Chris Hunt")) telling the story of the Beatles – one thousand days at a time. Featuring contributions from many of the world's leading rock critics and Beatles experts, such as [Hunter Davies](/wiki/Hunter_Davies "Hunter Davies"), [Mark Lewisohn](/wiki/Mark_Lewisohn "Mark Lewisohn"), [Richard Williams](/wiki/Richard_Williams_(journalist) "Richard Williams (journalist)"), [Ian MacDonald](/wiki/Ian_MacDonald "Ian MacDonald"), [Peter Doggett](/wiki/Peter_Doggett "Peter Doggett"), and [Alan Clayson](/wiki/Alan_Clayson "Alan Clayson"), the three magazines were published between 2002 and 2003, before being collected together by editor-in-chief Paul Trynka and published as the book *The Beatles: Ten Years That Shook The World* (Dorling Kindersley, 2004).

Other special editions have focused on [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd"), [psychedelia](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), and the [1960s](/wiki/1960s_in_music "1960s in music"). *Mojo* has also published four editions of "The MOJO Collection: The Greatest Albums Of All Time" ([Canongate Books](/wiki/Canongate_Books "Canongate Books")), originally edited by the magazine's founding features editor, [Jim Irvin](/wiki/Jim_Irvin "Jim Irvin"), and a series of short, definitive biographies under the imprint Mojo Heroes, starting in 2002 with *Neil Young: Reflections In Broken Glass*, written by [Sylvie Simmons](/wiki/Sylvie_Simmons "Sylvie Simmons"), a longtime *Mojo* contributing editor.

## Mojo Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mojo_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mojo Radio")]

The company behind the magazine, Bauer, also produced a digital [radio station](/wiki/Radio_station "Radio station").[[6]](#cite_note-6) This station was called Mojo Radio, and was transmitted on the [digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") networks in the UK ([Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)") channel 721 and [Sky Digital](/wiki/Sky_Digital_(UK) "Sky Digital (UK)") channel 0182, though not [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media "Virgin Media")) and online. The output of the station was based on that of the magazine. It was announced on 5 November 2008 that Mojo Radio would cease broadcasting on 30 November 2008, in order to save Bauer the financial outlay.

## *Mojo Rocks*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mojo_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mojo Rocks")]

The magazine's former editor-in-chief, Phil Alexander, had a regular show on the UK digital radio station [Planet Rock](/wiki/Planet_Rock_(radio_station) "Planet Rock (radio station)") entitled *Mojo Rocks*, in which he followed a *Mojo*-inspired playlist.
